g.data-narration: Welcome to Logic Equivalence. We explore when two propositions share identical truth values under all interpretations.
g.text: Logic Equivalence and Laws of Logic

g.data-narration: Two propositions are logically equivalent when they produce identical truth values across all possible interpretations.
g.text: Definition: Two propositions p and q are
identical truth values under ALL possible interpretations.

g.data-narration: Every proposition is either always true (tautology), always false (contradiction), or varies with input (contingency).
We use five connectives: negation flips truth, conjunction needs both true, disjunction needs at least one.
g.text: ¬p Negation: True when p is false
p ∧ q Conjunction: True ONLY when both true
p ∨ q Disjunction: True when at least one true
p → q Implication: False only when p=T, q=F
p ↔ q Biconditional: True when same truth value

g.data-narration: Identity laws show T and F act as neutral elements. Domination laws show T makes OR always true, F makes AND always false.
Idempotent laws show repeated operations yield the same result. Commutative swaps operands; Associative regroups them.
Distributive laws show how AND distributes over OR and vice versa. These are essential for simplifying complex expressions.
De Morgan's Laws are crucial: negating AND becomes OR of negations, and vice versa. This is frequently tested!
Absorption laws show elegant simplifications: p AND (p OR q) equals p, and p OR (p AND q) equals p.
Negation laws reveal contradictions and tautologies: p AND NOT p is always false; p OR NOT p is always true.
Implication p → q equals NOT p OR q. Its contrapositive NOT q → NOT p is logically equivalent.
Truth tables prove equivalence by showing identical columns. Here p→q and ¬p∨q match perfectly across all rows.
g.text: Truth Table: Proving p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q

g.data-narration: We simplify ¬(p∨q)∨(p∧q) step by step using De Morgan's, distributive, and negation laws to reach p∨¬q.
Exam success requires memorizing all laws, converting implications early, and verifying with truth tables when uncertain.
g.text: Memorize ALL logical equivalence laws - essential for quick problem-solving
Use truth tables for verification when unsure - foolproof method
REMEMBER De Morgan's Laws - frequently tested, often misapplied
Convert p→q to ¬p∨q immediately - simplifies most expressions

g.data-narration: We covered logical equivalence, key laws including De Morgan's, implications, truth tables, and simplification techniques.
g.text: • Logical Equivalence: p ≡ q means identical truth values in ALL interpretations
• Key Laws: Identity, Domination, Idempotent, Commutative, Associative, Distributive,
De Morgan's, Absorption, Negation - Master them all!
